findings.summary: Best first adoption candidate for offline or host-owned compression of natural-language and large tool-output context, provided system prompts, recent turns, structured JSON, generated metadata, and tool envelopes are excluded.
Technically broad runtime support exists, but proxy/wrap mutates provider traffic and environment, which is too high blast-radius for core spec-kit gates and hooks.
Headroom MCP can be useful as an explicit compression/retrieval helper, but must not replace mk-spec-memory, mk-skill-advisor, or mk-code-index tool contracts.
They create a parallel memory/instruction plane and write AGENTS.md or native memory files, conflicting with Spec Kit Memory as the single memory source of truth.
CacheAligner is now detector-only and explicitly avoids prompt mutation, making it compatible with prompt-cache diagnostics.

findings.verdict: do not adopt for core runtime
findings.summary: It appends system-prompt instructions and lowers effort on mechanical turns; useful experimentally, but conflicts with constitutional voice, prompt immutability, and deep-loop reasoning guarantees.

rankedRecommendation: Pilot library compress() in an offline benchmark harness over copied deep-loop artifacts and large natural-language tool outputs.
Adopt CacheAligner only as detector/logging signal if needed.
Evaluate Headroom MCP as an explicit manual helper with a non-default server name and no auto-registration.
Evaluate RTK directly or through Headroom only for shell-output shortening.
Do not adopt proxy, wrap, output-shaper, headroom learn, or cross-agent memory into the core stack without a separate guarded experiment.
